title: Broadcom Jumps 5% After Hours on Earnings Beat, AI Outlook
content:
There was good news across the board from Broadcom after the close on Wednesday with the chipmaker reporting better-than-expected earnings and revenue, and issuing a strong forecast for the current period. During a period of broad-based uncertainty due to the war in the Middle East and the reshaping of the technology industry, the results are a great example of how this chipmaker has become one of the most prominent beneficiaries of the artificial infrastructure buildout.

Without going into great detail, you don’t have to be a CFA to know that revenue, up 30% compared to the same period a year earlier is good for the company. Net income also posted a meaningful increase year over year. Broadcom’s adjusted earnings were a little ahead of consensus estimates, signaling a pattern of disciplined execution that investors have come to expect from the company.

However, standing out amongst all the numbers in the earnings report was AI-related revenue. It more than doubled compared to last year, prompting CEO Hock Tan to credit the performance to robust demand for custom AI-accelerators and AI networking products. Tan also said that the result fulfilled a target he had set publicly several months earlier.

Here’s the best part for a technology market that has been screaming recently for solid guidance. Tan signaled that Broadcom’s AI semiconductor revenue is expected to climb even further in the current quarter, citing continued momentum in demand from hyperscale cloud customers.

In case you were wondering what Broadcom actually does in the AI space, think of it as the behind the scenes partner that helps the big tech companies turn their custom chip ideas into real products. It doesn’t make the chips itself, but it provides the intellectual property and the technology that gets those designs ready for the fabrication plant. Not glamorous, but absolutely essential.

Broadcom is also stepping up its competition with Amazon, Google, Meta and Microsoft, all of whom are racing to build their own proprietary chips to support their AI ambitions. That’s a big pond to be swimming in.

Buyback Program Signals Confidence in What Lies Ahead

If the earnings and outlook weren’t enough, Broadcom’s board also authorized a major new share buyback program running through the end of next year. When a company buys back its own stock, it usually means they like what they see ahead.

James Hyerczyk is a U.S. based seasoned technical analyst and educator with over 40 years of experience in market analysis and trading, specializing in chart patterns and price movement. He is the author of two books on technical analysis and has a background in both futures and stock markets.

title: Marvell Gets a Fresh Bid as AI Infrastructure Spending Lifts Revenue Visibility
content:
Marvell Technology (NASDAQ:MRVL) is making headlines this Friday morning after a blowout Q4 earnings report sent shares surging sharply in after-hours trading on Thursday, March 5, 2026. The semiconductor company not only topped Wall Street’s estimates for the quarter but issued forward guidance that dramatically exceeded analyst expectations, fueled by an accelerating boom in AI-driven data center infrastructure.

With Big Tech giants pouring hundreds of billions into AI buildouts, Marvell finds itself squarely in the crosshairs of one of the most powerful secular growth trends in the technology sector. Investors are now asking whether this earnings-driven rally marks a turning point for a stock that had underperformed the broader semiconductor group over the prior two quarters.

Marvell delivered a strong Q4 for fiscal year 2026, reporting revenue of $2.22 billion, a 22% jump year-over-year, edging past analyst estimates of $2.21 billion. Adjusted earnings per share came in at $0.80, a penny ahead of the consensus estimate of $0.79, continuing a pattern of beats that has defined much of the company’s recent fiscal year.

CEO Matt Murphy highlighted that Marvell posted record full-year fiscal 2026 revenue of $8.195 billion, representing 42% growth year-over-year, driven by what he called “robust AI demand.” Non-GAAP EPS for the full year reached $2.84, an 81% increase year-over-year, underscoring the operating leverage embedded in the business model.

Murphy also noted that design wins in fiscal 2026 hit an all-time record, a forward-looking signal that growth momentum is far from peaking. Looking into Q1 FY2027, Marvell guided for revenue of approximately $2.40 billion, plus or minus 5%, well above the analyst consensus of $2.27 to $2.28 billion, and adjusted EPS of $0.79 versus estimates of $0.74.

The most striking element of Thursday’s report was Marvell’s long-range revenue outlook, which sent shares surging roughly 15% in extended trading. The company now expects fiscal 2028 revenue to grow nearly 40% and approach $15 billion, a figure that towers over the analyst consensus estimate of $12.92 billion compiled by LSEG.

Marvell also raised its fiscal 2027 revenue forecast to more than 30% growth year-over-year, nearing $11 billion, up from prior expectations of approximately $10 billion. President and COO Chris Koopmans attributed the confidence to visibility into hyperscaler capital spending plans and a record pace of bookings, stating the company feels “very confident” in hitting those numbers.

The optimism is well-grounded: Big Tech firms including Alphabet, Microsoft, Amazon, and Meta are expected to collectively spend at least $630 billion on AI infrastructure in 2026 alone, lifting demand for the custom ASICs and high-speed interconnect products that are core to Marvell’s data center franchise. Marvell also completed its $3.25 billion acquisition of Celestial AI — a photonic fabrics company — which is expected to further differentiate its chip-to-chip connectivity offering as AI clusters continue to scale.

As of pre-market trading on March 6, 2026 at approximately 5:03 AM EST, MRVL shares were trading at $84.49, up $8.81 or +11.64%, after closing the prior session at $75.68 — itself down 3.09% on the day before the earnings release. The stock’s 52-week range spans $47.09 to $102.77, and the current analyst average price target stands at $114.06, with the high target reaching $150.00, suggesting significant upside from current levels even after the premarket pop.

On a trailing basis, MRVL carries a P/E ratio of 26.65 and a forward P/E of 23.26, with a market cap of approximately $66.1 billion intraday. From a performance perspective, the stock has lagged year-to-date at -10.88% versus the S&P 500’s -0.22%, and is down 15.76% over the past year against the index’s +16.91% gain, but has outperformed over three and five years, up 73.75% and 86.01% respectively.

Analyst sentiment remains broadly constructive; Evercore ISI Group maintained its Outperform rating as recently as March 4, though it trimmed its price target from $156 to $133. Thursday’s results and upgraded outlook may prompt a fresh wave of price target revisions across the Street.

title: Marvell Stock Flying as Analysts Raise Price Targets En Masse
content:
Marvell shares (NASDAQ:MRVL) jumped 17% early on Friday, after the chipmaker delivered a strong earnings beat and sharply raised its multi-year revenue outlook, prompting a wave of Wall Street upgrades that underscore growing confidence in the company’s position across AI data center infrastructure.

The semiconductor name is currently trading up 17.5% following its fiscal fourth-quarter results and guidance that exceeded expectations across the board. The rally added roughly $12 billion to Marvell’s market capitalisation and pushed the stock to fresh highs, reflecting renewed enthusiasm for companies supplying the picks and shovels of the artificial intelligence buildout.

Marvell reported fiscal Q4 2026 revenue of $2.22 billion, up 22% year-over-year and 7% sequentially, with non-GAAP earnings per share of $0.80 representing 33% annual growth. Both metrics topped consensus estimates. More importantly, the company guided fiscal Q1 2027 revenue to $2.4 billion at the midpoint, representing 27% year-over-year growth and roughly 8% sequential expansion, again ahead of Street expectations.

The real catalyst, however, came from management’s upgraded multi-year outlook.

Marvell now expects fiscal 2027 revenue to grow more than 30% year-over-year to nearly $11 billion, driven by data center revenue growth of approximately 40%. Within that, the company’s interconnect business, spanning optical DSPs, active electrical cables, and retimers, is projected to grow more than 50% year-over-year. For fiscal 2028, management outlined a path to nearly $15 billion in revenue, well above the prior Street consensus of around $13 billion.

The company also raised its calendar 2027 data center revenue growth outlook from 40% to 50% year-over-year, with custom compute revenue, AI accelerators and ASICs designed for hyperscale customers, expected to double. Management indicated the company is on track to deliver approximately 40% total revenue growth with more than $5 in non-GAAP earnings power, significantly improving medium-term visibility into the business.

Data center revenue reached $1.65 billion in the fiscal fourth quarter, up 21% year-over-year and 9% sequentially, driven by AI demand across optical interconnects for 800 Gigabit PAM4 and early 1.6 Terabit deployments, custom AI silicon programs, and switching and storage products. The company’s switch revenue is expected to more than double from over $300 million in fiscal 2026 to over $600 million in fiscal 2027, while custom revenue already doubled in fiscal 2026 with strong growth expected to continue.

The earnings report triggered a flurry of analyst action.

Bank of America upgraded Marvell to Buy from Neutral and raised its price target to $110 from $90, citing greater confidence in the company’s leverage to AI optical connectivity, the potential for success in the upcoming Microsoft custom chip program, and improved visibility on an Amazon XPU transition. The firm raised its fiscal 2027 and 2028 sales forecasts by 8% and 12%, and its pro-forma EPS estimates by 15% and 12%, to $3.82 and $5.43 respectively.

Evercore ISI analyst Mark Lipacis lifted his price target to $155 from $133 while maintaining an Outperform rating, calling the results and guidance a “healthy raise” consistent with the firm’s channel checks. Rosenblatt raised its target to $140 from $115, highlighting improved visibility and characterising demand from hyperscale data centers as “insatiable.” The firm noted that multiple areas within hyperscale infrastructure are driving the revenue outlook upside, not just one or two products.

RBC Capital increased its price target to $115 from $105, keeping an Outperform rating and noting that management sees growth accelerating through fiscal 2027. The firm emphasised that optical momentum remains strong and should sustain through the year on 800G demand and 1.6T/ZR+ upgrade cycles. RBC noted that Marvell trades at roughly 23 times calendar 2026 earnings with a very low price-to-earnings-growth ratio of approximately 0.1, arguing the risk-reward remains attractive.

JPMorgan raised its target to $135 from $130 with an Overweight rating, stating the firm is “impressed” with Marvell’s multi-year revenue outlook and the diversity of its customer program ramps. B. Riley lifted its target to $135 from $130, highlighting strong momentum in data center’s custom compute, optics, and switch businesses, as well as scaling XPU and XPU attach programs.

Even more cautious voices acknowledged the improved fundamentals.

Goldman Sachs raised its price target to $100 from $90 while keeping a Neutral rating, expecting the stock to trade higher following the stronger guidance and constructive data center outlook. Morgan Stanley increased its target to $103 from $95 with an Equal Weight rating, noting that results were strong, guidance moved higher again, and management sounded incrementally confident in the durability of the cycle, with near-term strength aligning with what the firm has been hearing across the AI infrastructure supply chain.

The upgraded outlook reflects Marvell’s positioning across multiple layers of AI infrastructure. The company supplies optical interconnects that move data between AI accelerators at speeds of 800 Gigabits and 1.6 Terabits per second, custom ASICs designed specifically for Microsoft Azure and Amazon Web Services cloud platforms, Ethernet switches that route traffic within data centers, and storage controllers. This diversified exposure within the AI stack differentiates Marvell from pure-play GPU or networking vendors.

The custom compute business represents a particularly important growth vector. Marvell designs bespoke AI accelerators for hyperscale customers seeking alternatives to off-the-shelf GPUs, allowing cloud providers to optimise performance and cost for their specific workloads. The company has confirmed that programs with both Microsoft and Amazon remain on track, with AWS Trainium 3 and Microsoft Maia chips moving to 3-nanometer manufacturing processes.

Looking ahead, Marvell’s next test will be executing on the upgraded fiscal 2027 and 2028 targets. The company must successfully ramp 1.6 Terabit optical products, deliver on custom ASIC programs for Microsoft and Amazon, and continue gaining share in Ethernet switching, all while managing supply chain constraints and maintaining competitive positioning against rivals including Broadcom, Nvidia, and Intel. Management’s increased confidence suggests internal visibility has improved, but translating design wins into revenue at the scale implied by guidance will require flawless execution.

The analyst community has clearly signalled greater conviction in Marvell’s AI infrastructure story, with price targets now spanning from $100 to $155 and a consensus around $115-120. The upgrade wave from firms including Bank of America, which moved from Neutral to Buy, indicates that sceptics are becoming believers as evidence mounts that AI-driven data center demand is not only strong but accelerating through fiscal 2027 and 2028. Whether this optimism proves prescient or premature will depend on the durability of hyperscale AI spending and Marvell’s ability to capitalise on what management characterises as a multi-year infrastructure buildout, balanced against the risks inherent in any business concentrated among a handful of customers navigating the uncertainties of an emerging technology cycle.
